pattern: Choosing at most one cell per row with all-distinct values is exactly a bipartite matching between distinct values and rows, and matching the largest values first with augmenting paths greedily builds the optimal selection.
hints: Ignore individual cells for a moment and think in terms of distinct values: picking a value means claiming exactly one row that contains it, and no row can be claimed twice.
This is a bipartite matching problem between distinct values and rows; process the values from largest to smallest and try to match each one to some row containing it.

Use augmenting paths (as in classic bipartite matching): if a value's candidate rows are all taken, see whether the row's current value can instead be rematched to a different row it also appears in, freeing up a row for the new value.

edge_inputs.description: three rows where one value conflict must be resolved by augmenting
two rows sharing a value that must be reassigned to fit both high values in
every cell shares the same value across every row
a row entirely of duplicate values only ever contributes once
the best-looking greedy pick blocks a better overall combination unless it augments

stress.description: grids up to the full 10x10 boundary with a small value alphabet to force conflicts
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 10))
    n = rng.randint(1, 10)
    grid = [[rng.randint(1, 8) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]
stress.note: a narrow value range forces many rows to compete for the same values

stress.description: grids with a wide value alphabet, fewer conflicts
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 10))
    n = rng.randint(1, 10)
    grid = [[rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]
stress.note: checks the matching still behaves when conflicts are rare
